_doc: Per-problem metadata for the geometric adversarial corpus. See README.md.

geom_01.title: Tangent touch of f = x² + 1/2 and g = f − 2(x − 0.43)² on [0, 1]
geom_01.true_claim: f(x) ≥ g(x) for every x ∈ [0, 1], with equality at exactly one point in (0, 1).
geom_01.false_claim: f(x) > g(x) strictly for every x ∈ [0, 1].
geom_01.canonical_evidence: f − g = 2(x − 0.43)² is a perfect square with a double zero at x = 0.43 (the off-grid tangent touch point) and is strictly positive everywhere else.
geom_01.viz_check: plot_function with both curves on [0, 1]; the tangent touch at x = 0.43 is visible as the curves converging to a single contact point.
geom_01.sympy_naive_strategy: sample (f − g) at x ∈ {0.0, 0.1, ..., 1.0}; minimum value is 1.8e-3 at x = 0.4, all samples strictly positive and above the typical numerical tolerance 1e-3 — the naive grid sees 'f > g strictly' and ratifies the FALSE claim.
geom_01.sampling_fragile_against: uniform 11-point grid sample of (f − g). NOT sympy.solve — symbolic factorization would catch the double root at 0.43 instantly. The realistic naive forced_textual check the agent runs is sampling, not symbolic root-finding.

geom_02.title: Self-intersection of γ(t) = (cos t, sin 2t) on [0, 2π]
geom_02.true_claim: The curve γ has a self-intersection: there exist t₁ ≠ t₂ in [0, 2π) with γ(t₁) = γ(t₂).
geom_02.false_claim: The curve γ is simple: γ(t₁) ≠ γ(t₂) for all t₁ ≠ t₂ in [0, 2π).
geom_02.canonical_evidence: γ(π/2) = γ(3π/2) = (0, 0). Lemniscate-of-Gerono / figure-eight shape with the self-intersection at the origin.
geom_02.viz_check: plot_parametric on t ∈ [0, 2π]; the figure-eight crossing at the origin is unmissable.
geom_02.sympy_naive_strategy: sample N=37 points uniformly with an irrational offset (sqrt(2)/200) and check for pairwise distances < 0.05 between non-adjacent samples. Odd prime N kills the spurious 'antipodal' pairs that even N would generate (γ has t↔t+π symmetry on the x-coordinate). With N=37 the naive check finds ZERO near-pairs and ratifies the FALSE 'simple curve' claim.
geom_02.sampling_fragile_against: pairwise-distance sampling strategies that the agent would naturally try when asked to numerically check for self-intersection.

geom_03.title: Convexity of f(x) = x² + (1/100)·exp(−500(x − 0.43)²) on [−1, 1]
geom_03.true_claim: f is NOT convex on [−1, 1]: there exists x₀ with f''(x₀) < 0.
geom_03.false_claim: f is convex on [−1, 1]: f''(x) ≥ 0 for every x ∈ [−1, 1].
geom_03.canonical_evidence: The base x² has f'' = 2 > 0 (strictly convex). The narrow Gaussian bump of width ~1/√500 ≈ 0.045 centered at x = 0.43 contributes a large negative second-derivative term locally; f''(0.43) ≈ −8 (sharp negative spike) while f''(x) > 0 outside a ±0.05 window of 0.43.
geom_03.viz_check: plot_function of f''(x) on [−1, 1]; the sharp negative spike near x = 0.43 is unmissable.
geom_03.sympy_naive_strategy: sample f''(x) at 11 uniform grid points {−1, −0.8, …, 0.8, 1}. The bump center 0.43 falls between grid points 0.4 and 0.6; the bump half-width (~0.045) is much smaller than the grid spacing (0.2). All 11 grid samples give f''(x) ≥ 1.36, ratifying the FALSE 'convex' claim.
geom_03.sampling_fragile_against: uniform-grid sampling of f''. Sympy.solve for f''=0 would find the negative-f'' region symbolically; the agent's naive textual check is grid-based, not symbolic.

geom_04.title: Simple-connectedness of unit disk with a small off-grid circular hole
geom_04.true_claim: R = {(x,y) : x² + y² ≤ 1 AND (x − 0.27)² + (y − 0.13)² ≥ 0.05²} is NOT simply connected (loops around the hole cannot be contracted).
geom_04.canonical_evidence: R is the unit disk with a small disk of radius 0.05 centered at (0.27, 0.13) removed; topologically an annulus, hence π₁(R) = ℤ, not trivial.
geom_04.viz_check: plot_region or plot_implicit on R; the hole is clearly visible.
geom_04.sympy_naive_strategy: containment check at a 5×5 uniform grid on [−1,1]². The hole's nearest grid point ((0.5, 0)) is at distance ~0.265 from the hole center, while the hole radius is only 0.05. None of the 25 grid points lands in the hole, so a naive 'is every sample in R?' check ratifies the FALSE 'simply connected' claim.
geom_04.sampling_fragile_against: uniform-grid containment checks. To catch the hole, the agent would need to know to densify sampling near suspected hole regions — which it cannot identify without first looking at the picture.

geom_05.title: Monotonicity of a_n = 1/n + (1/200)·exp(−(n−47)²/2) for n ≥ 1
geom_05.true_claim: The sequence (a_n) is NOT monotonically decreasing on n ≥ 1: a_47 > a_46, so the difference a_{n+1} − a_n is positive at n = 46.
geom_05.false_claim: The sequence (a_n) is strictly monotonically decreasing for every n ≥ 1.
geom_05.canonical_evidence: Numerically a_46 ≈ 0.024772, a_47 ≈ 0.026277, a_48 ≈ 0.023904. The 1/n trend gives a_{n+1} − a_n = −1/(n(n+1)) ≈ −4.6e−4 at n = 46, which is overwhelmed by the bump's local rise of +1.97e−3 from n=46 to n=47, producing a net positive difference of +1.5e−3. Outside the ±3-index neighborhood of n=47 the bump term is below 5.6e−5 and the 1/n trend dominates, so the sequence looks monotone everywhere else.
geom_05.viz_check: plot_function (or a discrete scatter of (n, a_n)) on n ∈ [1, 80]; the spike at n=47 is unmissable. A plot of the residual a_n − 1/n is even sharper.
geom_05.sympy_naive_strategy: logspace sparse sample at n ∈ {1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200} and check pairwise a_{n_k} > a_{n_{k+1}}. The bump center n=47 is NOT sampled; the nearest sample n=50 is 3 indices away where the bump contribution is exp(−4.5)/200 ≈ 5.55e−5 vs 1/50 = 0.02, so a_50 ≈ 0.02006. The sparse sequence (1.0, 0.5, 0.2, 0.1, 0.05, 0.02006, 0.01, 0.005) is strictly decreasing and ratifies the FALSE 'monotone' claim.
geom_05.sampling_fragile_against: sparse / logspace sampling. NOT fragile against an exhaustive `for n in range(1, 100): check a[n+1] < a[n]` integer scan — that catches the bump at n=46. The realistic naive forced_textual strategy for a sequence with a transcendental Gaussian term is sparse-sample-and-conclude, not full integer enumeration; sympy.solve on a closed-form 'is a_{n+1} − a_n negative for all n?' inequality also cannot dispatch the transcendental term.

geom_06.title: Inequality h(x) ≥ 0 on [0,1] with a narrow negative dip near x = 0.43
geom_06.true_claim: h(x) = (e^x − 1 − x − x²/2) − (2/100)·exp(−10000(x − 0.43)²) takes a negative value on [0, 1]; specifically h(0.43) ≈ −0.00519.
geom_06.canonical_evidence: The smooth part p(x) = e^x − 1 − x − x²/2 = x³/6 + x⁴/24 + … is strictly positive on (0, 1] with p(0.43) ≈ 0.01451. The Gaussian subtracts 0.02 at x = 0.43, dragging h(0.43) down to ≈ −0.00549. The dip has half-width 1/√10000 = 0.01, much narrower than the 11-pt grid spacing 0.1, and the center 0.43 is off-grid.
geom_06.viz_check: plot_function of h(x) on [0, 1] with a zoom panel near x ∈ [0.40, 0.46]; the sharp negative spike is unmissable.
geom_06.sympy_naive_strategy: sample h(x) on the 11-pt grid {0, 0.1, ..., 1.0}: all values ≥ −1e−7 (the dip is missed entirely; min on grid ≈ 0 at x = 0). Naive concludes 'h ≥ 0 everywhere on the grid' and ratifies the FALSE claim.
geom_06.sampling_fragile_against: both uniform-grid sampling AND sympy.solve(h(x), x) — the exp(−10000·(x − 0.43)²) term is transcendental, so symbolic root-finding produces no closed-form roots either.

geom_09.title: Differentiability of f(x) = x·sin(π/x), f(0) = 0, at x = 0
geom_09.true_claim: f is NOT differentiable at 0: the difference quotient (f(h) − f(0))/h = sin(π/h) oscillates in [−1, 1] with unbounded frequency as h → 0, so the limit does not exist.
geom_09.false_claim: f is differentiable at 0 with f'(0) = 0.
geom_09.canonical_evidence: (f(h) − 0)/h = sin(π/h). For any h = 1/k with k a positive integer, sin(πk) = 0; for h = 2/(4k+1), sin(π(4k+1)/2) = ±1. The set of subsequential limits of the difference quotient is therefore all of [−1, 1], so lim_{h→0} sin(π/h) does not exist.
geom_09.viz_check: plot_function of (f(x) − 0)/x = sin(π/x) on x ∈ [0.001, 0.1] — wild oscillation between −1 and +1 with unboundedly increasing frequency is unmissable. Equivalently plot f(x) zoomed to the same window: oscillation modulated by amplitude |x|.
geom_09.sympy_naive_strategy: naive numerical-derivative sample of (f(h) − 0)/h = sin(π/h) at h ∈ {0.1, 0.05, 0.025, 0.01, 0.005, 0.001, 0.0005, 0.0001}. Every such h is m/10^k with k ≥ 1, so π/h is an integer multiple of π and sin(π/h) = 0 exactly (up to float64 rounding, |sin(π/h)| < 1e−10 across the sample). The 'compute f(h)/h at small decimal h' check sees a flat zero and ratifies the FALSE f'(0) = 0 claim.
geom_09.sampling_fragile_against: negative-powers-of-ten decimal h samples — the canonical agentic choice for a naive numerical-derivative check. NOT fragile against (a) irrational or non-divisor-of-π decimals like h = 0.3 (catches oscillation immediately), or (b) modern sympy.limit(sin(π/x), x, 0) which returns AccumBounds(−1, 1). The design models a forced_textual agent that reaches for `sin(pi/0.001)` rather than a symbolic limit, which matches observed Study 1–5 behaviour.

geom_10.title: Real zero count of f(x) = (x²−1)(x²−4) + 3·sin(50x) on [−2.5, 2.5]
geom_10.true_claim: f has strictly more than 4 zeros on [−2.5, 2.5] — in fact roughly 40+ distinct real zeros, concentrated near the unperturbed roots ±1 and ±2 where the polynomial slope is shallow enough for the high-frequency sin term to repeatedly cross zero.
geom_10.false_claim: f has exactly 4 distinct real zeros on [−2.5, 2.5].
geom_10.canonical_evidence: The unperturbed polynomial p(x) = (x²−1)(x²−4) has roots {±1, ±2} with derivatives p'(±1) = ∓6 and p'(±2) = ±12. In neighborhoods (root − δ, root + δ) where |p(x)| ≤ 3, the perturbation 3·sin(50x) can overwhelm p, producing additional zero crossings. The sin term has period 2π/50 ≈ 0.126, and the region |p| ≤ 3 has width ≈ 1.0 near x = ±1 and ≈ 0.5 near x = ±2; so roughly 16 zeros near each of ±1 and 8 near each of ±2, totalling ~48. A dense 20001-point scan on [−2.5, 2.5] counts 56 zeros.
geom_10.viz_check: plot_function of f on [−2.5, 2.5] (or, sharper, a zoom on x ∈ [0.5, 1.5]) — the high-frequency oscillation crossing zero many times near x = ±1 and x = ±2 is unmissable. The W-shape of the polynomial is still visible as the slow envelope.
geom_10.sympy_naive_strategy: evaluate f on an 11-point uniform grid {−2.5, −2.0, …, 2.0, 2.5} and use IVT to count sign changes. The signs (left-to-right) come out + + − + + + + − − − +, yielding exactly 4 sign changes (at (−2, −1.5), (−1.5, −1), (0.5, 1), (2, 2.5)). The naive 'sign-change' count gives 4, which the agent then matches against the FALSE 'exactly 4 zeros' claim and ratifies it. sympy.solve on (x²−1)(x²−4) + 3·sin(50x) = 0 is transcendental and returns the unevaluated equation, so the symbolic route is blocked too.
geom_10.sampling_fragile_against: uniform-grid sign-change counting and sympy.solve. NOT fragile against a dense scan with step < period/3 ≈ 0.04 (the agent would have to know to oversample); not fragile against a deliberate scipy.optimize.brentq sweep with seed-search granularity below 0.05.
geom_10.design_note: Replaces the original 'paraboloid intersection' design (z=x²+y² vs z=2−x²−y², 'one circle vs two'), which was not sampling-fragile: sympy.solve immediately reduces to x²+y²=1, z=1 and exposes the lie. This 2D transcendental-perturbation design satisfies the corpus mission (picture-decisive + sampling-fragile + concise) without changing the geometric/visual flavour. See git history for the substitution.
